title: The Weigold--Goldman Conjecture for Compact Lie Groups
speaker: Tsachik Gelander
abstract: Let n greater than 2. Weigold conjectured that if G is a finite simple group then the product replacement graph on n-generating tuples is connected (which also implies that it is an expander for n greater than 3). This conjecture is still (wide) open. I will discuss analog problems when G is a compact Lie group. More precisely, I will present the little that I know and speculate about stronger conjectures that I cannot prove.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Finite product-replacement action and Weigold transitivity
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with epimorphisms F_n -> G to a finite simple group, identifies them with generating n-tuples, describes the Nielsen action/product-replacement graph, and states Weigold's conjecture that for n >= 3 this graph is connected, with later comments on the large redundant component in finite approximations.

proof_stages.label: Redundancy and primitive-element reformulation
proof_stages.summary: After explaining the n=2 obstruction, redundant epimorphisms and primitive elements P_n of F_n are introduced, giving the reformulation that Weigold is equivalent to every epimorphism satisfying rho(P_n)=G, together with a weaker baby Weigold fixed-point/characteristic-quotient version.

proof_stages.label: Compact Lie-group representation space and Haar invariance
proof_stages.summary: For compact connected Lie groups G, the space Hom(F_n,G) is G^n with product Haar measure, and Nielsen moves preserve this measure, leading to the Goldman ergodicity statement for n >= 3.

proof_stages.label: Generic redundancy proof of Haar ergodicity
proof_stages.summary: The proof uses the full-measure set of pairs generating a dense subgroup and applies Fubini to an invariant set A, whose nontrivial fiber would be invariant under a dense subgroup acting ergodically on G, forcing A to have measure 0 or 1.

proof_stages.label: Algebraic ergodic measures
proof_stages.summary: Closed compact subgroups H <= G provide algebraic invariant measures supported on H^n, and the proposed classification says that every ergodic Nielsen-invariant measure on G^n should arise in this algebraic way.

proof_stages.label: Dense-redundancy conjecture and large-n evidence
proof_stages.summary: A stronger compact analogue asserts that every dense representation F_n -> G is redundant, equivalently that primitive images are dense; it would imply the measure-classification conjecture, and for fixed G with n large it follows by Nielsen-moving entries near the identity and using Lie algebra generation.

proof_stages.label: Baby compact Weigold and fixed-point constraints
proof_stages.summary: The baby compact conjecture asks for no fixed points on dense representations modulo Aut(G), equivalently no atomic ergodic invariant measures, and a hypothetical fixed point forces all primitive images into one finite-order conjugacy or characteristic-polynomial class, suggesting virtual solvability obstructions.

proof_stages.label: Zariski-linear analogues and conjugacy-class obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The final part formulates Zariski versions for representations into SL_d(C), especially that primitive unipotence should force a unipotent or virtually solvable image and hence constrain linear representations of Aut(F_n), and reduces part of the difficulty to understanding cosets gH contained in a single conjugacy class.

examples.name: Product replacement for random permutations
examples.note: The product-replacement/Nielsen graph is illustrated by random generation in large symmetric groups, such as shuffling cards, motivating connectedness and expansion without entering the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: product replacement for random permutations is described as motivation for random walks on generating tuples, with no concrete computation on a permutation example

examples.name: The n=2 trace-of-commutator obstruction
examples.note: For two generators in a matrix group, the trace of the commutator is a nonconstant Nielsen-invariant function, explaining why the main transitivity/ergodicity statements must exclude n=2.
examples.engagement_reason: the n=2 case is analyzed by introducing the trace-of-commutator function and checking its invariance under Nielsen transformations to obstruct transitivity

passes.reason: The rank-two case is the excluded boundary case, and the nonconstant trace-of-commutator invariant shows transitivity/ergodicity fails.

examples.name: Dense F4 representation into SL2(C) with non-dense primitive image
examples.note: A geometric construction of a dense representation F4 -> SL2(C) whose primitive images do not approach the identity shows that the ordinary topology analogue of primitive-density fails, motivating the Zariski formulation.
examples.engagement_reason: the dense F4 to SL2(C) representation with non-dense primitive image is cited as a known geometric example without constructing or analyzing it

examples.name: Regular unipotent primitive image
examples.note: The case where one primitive element maps to a regular unipotent is given as a tractable benchmark in which the broader primitive-unipotent/virtual-solvability conjecture can be proved.
examples.engagement_reason: regular unipotent primitive image is stated as a special case where a conjectural conclusion is known and regularity is defined, but no proof or concrete calculation is given
